data.title: Why Stock-Pickers Could Lead The Coming Decade
data.description:
Introduction

The current market rally is largely driven by hopes that AI will deliver significant productivity gains in the near future, but there are just as many challenges on the horizon. One of the most common narratives surrounding today’s environment is that the market is severely overvalued, which is supported by many traditional metrics, as we will get into later. A simple long-term chart of the S&P 500 (SPX) can itself become part of that narrative. It looks as if the index appears to have climbed steadily since 2008 with only minor setbacks, making it easy for investors to conclude that a major correction must be imminent, which can keep people out of the markets. This correction mongering is the narrative that gets the most attention online as it spikes fear, uncertainty, and doubt (FUD).

It is no secret that the S&P 500’s valuation is at multi-decade highs; in fact, at its highest levels since the early 1900s. This is true across nearly all widely used valuation metrics, including P/E ratio, forward P/E, P/B, P/S, and EV/EBITDA. Historically, such extremes have often preceded sharp corrections and typically imply lower forward returns when buying at today’s prices.

Future returns dependent on today's valuation become especially pronounced the longer one invests. As can be seen below, over the following 12 months, high equity valuations do not signify low returns, while the picture becomes much clearer regarding five- and 10-year returns, where clearly higher valuations today lead to much lower yields.

Today, I want to shed a different perspective on this issue, looking at relative valuation, potential broader economic AI gains, and stock-picking. Ultimately, I believe that while some companies are, the broader market is not yet valued for large AI gains, as most investors currently focus on the intermediaries, such as Nvidia, and not companies that will eventually turn large profits from AI services themselves. My theory is that rather than producing just a few winners, the economy will benefit as a whole. Singling out the Magnificent Seven and a few other extraordinarily highly valued stocks, there are many other less pricey and even cheap stocks that scouting for could present a better risk-to-reward ratio today than just buying the S&P 500 index.

A Note on Valuation

Much of the recent strength in the S&P 500 has come from the Magnificent Seven gaining market capitalization and, in turn, taking up a larger share of many passive investors’ portfolios. This development is not without justification, as earnings growth for these companies has surged at a pace far above the 493 other constituents. That leaves investors with two possible interpretations: either keep betting on the winners or take a contrarian stance and look elsewhere.

What supports the latter view is valuation, both relative and absolute. The standard S&P 500 index trades well above its historical average of around 17x earnings. In contrast, the equal-weighted S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:RSP) is only slightly above that average, showing little evidence of broad overvaluation. This suggests that the widespread online outrage about “the market being expensive” is largely misplaced.

Instead, only a select few stocks are commanding the premium that drives the index higher. Over time, however, AI’s impact could extend across the entire market. As adoption broadens and utility increases, many other companies stand to benefit from efficiency gains that are not yet priced in.

For this reason, I see the equal-weighted S&P 500 (RSP) as a compelling way to maintain exposure to U.S. growth at a fair valuation. To me, RSP is a Buy, while the S&P 500 is more appropriately rated a Hold.

Still, there may be an even better path than buying an index outright: identifying individual companies that combine strong current growth, attractive valuations, and compelling AI beneficiary stories.

Individual Stocks

As noted, a few stocks at the top of the indices currently trade at exceptionally high multiples. These companies do offer strong growth prospects, which partly justifies their elevated valuations. However, they also carry certain risks. A prime example is Nvidia (NVDA). The company recently reported its third consecutive quarter of slowing data center revenue growth, now at just around 5% quarter-over-quarter, a sign that expectations may be running ahead of reality.

At the same time, many other stocks present attractive opportunities, particularly in anticyclical sectors. Health care, for instance, is trading near historical lows relative to the broader market. In fact, current valuations are comparable to levels seen in the early 2000s.

Within this space, a few companies stand out as Buys, which I have already covered in previous analyses. These include my latest take on UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) and my first write-up on Elevance Health Inc. (ELV). Both companies combine solid growth, strong financial health, and exposure to high administrative costs that could be lowered from AI-driven efficiencies over time.

Risks

Even with the case for stock-picking and the RSP as a Buy, there are clear risks that investors must consider. First, market concentration remains a major factor, which means that the SPX’s performance is heavily influenced by a handful of large-cap stocks. If these stocks continue to outperform, RSP and a diversified stock-picking strategy could underperform relative to the index, even if the broader market rises.

Additionally, macroeconomic factors could impact the market unevenly. Year-over-year CPI inflation was 2.9% in August 2025, and any rapid increase in interest rates could compress valuations across sectors that RSP or a stock-picking strategy is overweight in. Trade disruptions or renewed tariffs could similarly affect smaller or mid-cap companies more than large-cap or mega-cap stocks.

Further, technological adoption and AI-driven gains may take longer to materialize than currently priced in by the market. If productivity improvements or cost reductions from AI are delayed, companies outside the Magnificent Seven may see slower earnings growth than expected, which would reduce the upside for RSP and stock-picked portfolios relative to the concentrated SPX.

Conclusion

In the long run, stocks always go up. However, it is important to remember that valuation does matter a lot when looking at returns over long time horizons. The overstated index bubble makes the QQQ today seem more attractive than the SPX, though analyzing and picking individual stocks should prove the most lucrative, as there are many undervalued gems out there that should also be able to benefit from AI efficiency gains and are not yet priced for it.

SPX: Maintain Hold / Cautious Buy or DCA.

QQQ: Initiate Buy.

data.title: I Don't Look At My Stock Prices - August Dividend Income Report
data.description:
In 2016, I made a life-changing decision: I took a sabbatical, put my family in a small RV, and drove to Costa Rica.

Upon my return in 2017, I officially quit my job as a private banker at National Bank and started working full-time on my baby: Dividend Stocks Rock. I also decided to manage my pension account held at the National Bank. I’ve built this portfolio publicly since 2017 to make a real-life case study. I decided to invest 100% of this money in dividend growth stocks.

In August 2017, I received $108,760.02 in a locked retirement account. This means I can’t add capital to the account, and growth is only generated through capital gains and dividends. I don’t report this portfolio’s results to brag about my returns or to tell you to follow my lead. I just want to share how I manage my portfolio monthly with all the good and the bad. I hope you can learn from my experience.

Avoiding Stock Price

Over the years, many members have sent me emails on the day their stock dropped by 10% or more. They typically ask me why the stock suddenly crashed. It could have been related to bad news, revised guidance, a DOJ lawsuit, etc.

I was more than once surprised to not know about the event before researching it, even when it was a stock I owned!

I’ll explain to you why later on in this article, but let’s first look at the results.

Performance in Review

Let’s start with the numbers as of September 1st, 2025 (before the bell):

Original amount invested in September 2017 (no additional capital added): $108,760.02.

  • Current portfolio value: $314,694.70
  • Dividends paid: $5,211.00 (TTM)
  • Average yield: 1.66%
  • 2024 performance: +26.00%
  • VFV.TO= +35.24%, XIU.TO = +20.72%
  • Dividend growth: +12.22%

Total return since inception (September 2017 – September 2025): 189.35%

Annualized return (since September 2017 – 94 months): 14.53%

Vanguard S&P 500 Index ETF (VFV:CA) annualized return (since Sept 2017): 16.08% (total return 221.50%)

iShares S&P/TSX 60 ETF (XIU:CA) annualized return (since Sept 2017): 11.97% (total return 142.50%)

I Don’t Look at My Stock Prices (!?!)

This summer, John, a PRO member, asked me how I decided to add Stantec (STN:CA) to my portfolio in September of last year at a price close to “perfection.”

When I looked at the stock price on that day, I realized it had risen by more than 30%, and I had purchased my shares at almost their lowest price over the past 12 months. Before that email from John…

I had not noticed the price I paid for the stock, and I had not noticed the current price either!

Avoiding stock price & stock return monitoring makes me a better investor

You already know that I don’t give much attention to the stock price of a company I want to buy. After completing my analysis, I will look at the average dividend yield and P/E ratio over the past 5 years vs. the current metrics. But besides that, I don’t do technical analysis nor wait to buy at a “better price”.

I’ve always believed that the “best price” is whatever the price is “today”. After 22 years of investing, I could say that this belief has served me well most of the time!

This reflex cuts through the paralysis by analysis like a knife into a mango in Costa Rica. It makes my investment process straightforward and easier to execute.

But I realize that I don’t look at “today’s price” when I buy pretty much all the time!

Over the years, many members have sent me emails on the day their stock dropped by 10% or more. They typically ask me why the stock suddenly crashed. It could have been related to bad news, reviewed guidance, DOJ lawsuit, etc.

I was more than once surprised to not know about the event before researching it, even when it was a stock I owned!

Is it because I’m not on top of things?

Quite the opposite.

I miss most big movements because I don’t stare at the daily fluctuation. However, I still monitor my portfolio quarterly with great attention.

Therefore, I avoid acting on emotions (the day you lose $5,000 on a stock is not the day you want to decide to act on those emotions).

I also cut through the noise, as short-term fluctuations don’t affect my mindset. You would be surprised to count the number of times some stocks go up or down by 10% in a year! Just for fun, Stantec moved by more than 10% over a few months (from peak to bottom or vice versa) 4 times in the past 12 months!

I can make decisions based on financial metrics and my investment thesis, as opposed to giving too much weight to short-term price volatility.

Plus, let’s be honest, once the stock is down 10%, it’s a bit late to react anyway! I can’t trade faster than BlackRock.

What I do instead of looking at the stock price

You probably know I’m a soccer coach in my free time. In soccer, you have eleven players on the field. If each of them plays their role, you’ll have a good game. Sometimes, your star player will underperform (or get injured!), but other players often stand up and compensate.

Will I be disappointed if my top scorer gets a yellow card and misses two empty nets in a game that we win 3-2? No, because other players have no doubt taken over and compensated.

As I report my pension plan monthly, you can be assured that I’m aware of whether my portfolio is doing well or not. However, I spend little to no time on individual positions. I’d rather look at my total portfolio performance instead of individual stock performance.

My portfolio is a team, and while some players let me down occasionally, the core will always make me smile over the course of the season.

I will look at my stock price performance briefly with my PRO report and then do a full review (including stock price performance) at the end of the year.

This process enables me to be more “stoic” in facing my portfolio. Most of the time, we make mistakes because our emotions drive our decisions. Not looking at the stock price is effectively protecting me from the noise ?.

Smith Manoeuvre Update

Slowly but surely, the portfolio is taking shape with 14 companies spread across 8 sectors. My goal is to build a portfolio of thriving companies with a solid dividend triangle (e.g., with positive revenue, EPS and dividend growth trends). The current portfolio yield is at 3.03% with a 5-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 10.92%.

  • The portfolio value is now at $23,990.76
  • The portfolio debt is at $19,750.
  • The annual income is $728.04
  • To report my Smith Manoeuvre, I export the Excel data from my DSR PRO dashboard.

Smith Manoeuvre Portfolio Summary

Here’s my SM portfolio summary as of September 1st, 2025 (before the bell):

Adding more shares of Great-West Lifeco (GWO:CA)

Great-West was among the first companies I added to my portfolio when I started the SM portfolio. This purchase will give it a little boost to be up to par with other positions. I’m trying to maintain a portfolio that is as equally weighted as possible.

Pension Portfolio Summary

Here’s my pension plan portfolio summary as of September 1st, 2025 (before the bell):

Total value: $314,694.70 (+$4,459.85 or +1.44% from last month).

There were still some companies reporting earnings in August.

Alimentation Couche-Tard’s fuel tank is running low.

Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD:CA) reported a weak quarter. Revenue was down 5%, but EPS fell 6%. Sales were hurt by weak fuel revenue, while same-store merchandise revenues increased by 0.4% in the U.S., 3.8% in Europe and other regions, and 4.1% in Canada. The decrease in adjusted EPS is primarily driven by lower road transportation fuel gross margins in the United States, the impacts of inflation on operating expenses and of strategic investments on depreciation. During the quarter, ATD closed the acquisition of 270 company-owned and operated convenience retail and fuel sites operating under the GetGo Café + Market (“GetGo”). At least, the 7-eleven saga is finally over.

National Bank did great… but investors didn’t like it

National Bank (NA:CA) reported another strong quarter, with revenue up 21% and adjusted EPS up 15%. The Personal & Commercial Segment was up 5%, fueled by the acquisition of CWB but partially offset by lower interest margins and higher PCLs. The Wealth segment was up 12% on higher fees. The Capital Markets segment was +5%, driven by corporate and investment banking revenues, while the U.S. & International Segment was up 13% on higher revenue and lower PCLs (lower for Credigy, partially offset by higher at ABA bank). PCLs increased by 36% to $203M for the quarter and by 146% to $1B for the first 9 months of the year.

Home Depot has built better quarters in the past

Home Depot (HD:CA) reported an okay quarter with revenue up 5% and EPS up 2%. Company comparable sales increased 1%, with the U.S. up 1.4%, where the average ticket rose about 1.4% while comparable transactions declined 0.4%, and foreign exchange reduced total company comparables by roughly 40 basis points. Management said momentum in smaller home improvement projects continued in the first half, which, alongside modestly positive comps, was the key top-line driver for this quarter. Adjusted operating margin was 14.8% versus 15.3%, reflecting higher SG&A. For 2025, Home Depot still expects approximately 2.8% total sales growth and about 1.0% comparable sales growth.

CCL Industries labelled more revenue

CCL Industries (CCL.A:CA) reported a good quarter with revenue up 5% and EPS up 8%. Growth was a mix of organic (2%), acquisitions (1%), and a 1.8% positive FX impact. By segment, CCL was up 7.9%, Avery was down 3.9%, Checkpoint was up 4.6% and Innovia was down 0.7%. Growth was broadest at the CCL segment, which delivered 4.7% organic sales growth, while Checkpoint rose on MAS product strength. Avery declined on softer back-to-school shipments and tariff costs, and Innovia was essentially flat overall amid stronger Americas results offset by European start-up costs. Profitability improved with operating income up 6.1%, aided by strong contributions from CCL, Checkpoint and Innovia.

Royal Bank is the king of banks

Royal Bank (RY:CA) reported a robust quarter with revenue up 16% and EPS up 18%. By segment: Personal banking was up 22% on higher revenue and interest margins. Commercial banking was up 2% as revenue growth was partially offset by higher PCLs. Wealth was up 15% on higher fees due to growing AUM. Insurance was up 45% due to improved life insurance claims experience. Capital Markets increased 13% on higher revenue in Global Markets and Corporate & Investment Banking. Total PCLs increased $222M (+34%) to $881M, primarily due to higher provisions in Capital Markets, Commercial Banking and Personal Banking. PCLs have increased 40% in the first 9 months of 2025 vs. 2024.

Dollarama isn’t selling cheap

Dollarama (DOL:CA) reported another solid quarter with revenue up 10% and EPS up 14%. Comparable store sales in Canada for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 increased by 4.9%, consisting of a 3.9% increase in the number of transactions and a 0.9% increase in average transaction size. The company has also completed the acquisition of The Reject Shop (395 stores in Australia), which will contribute to growth moving forward. Management won’t provide comps for Australia while they optimize operations. Share of Dollarcity net earnings rose to $38.3M for the April-June 2025 period with strong operations and store growth from 658 locations; the first Dollarcity store in Mexico opened recently.

Dividend Income: $406.54 (-10.86% vs August 2024)

August is down 11% vs. last year, mostly related to the sale of my shares in Texas Instruments (TXN) that paid a $65 USD dividend last year. Most of my holdings are now showing mid-single-digit dividend increases vs. last year.

Since I started this portfolio in September 2017, I have received a total of $32,421.17 CAD in dividends. Keep in mind that this is a “pure dividend growth portfolio” as no capital can be added to this account other than retained and/or reinvested dividends. Therefore, all dividend growth is coming from the stocks and not from any additional capital being added to the account.

Final Thoughts

The month of September will include several dividend payments. I should have enough cash (over $1,000) to add to my position in Dollarama. I don’t expect doing any other transactions for my pension account, but I’m thinking of selling my position in Canadian Tire (CTC.A:CA) to increase DOL.TO in my Smith Manoeuvre account. As I already mentioned in a previous update, my goal is to build a full position in DOL.TO (roughly 3% of all my portfolios). Slowly but surely!

In the meantime, I stay fully invested.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Raise Cash: Jerome Powell Sounds The Valuation Alarm On Stocks
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chairman, Jerome Powell gave a speech on Tuesday, September 23, and in it he stated that stock prices appear "fairly highly valued." This could be viewed as a serious warning or at least be meant to throw some cold water on all the excessive bullishness that we have seen in the stock market lately. I believe that Jerome Powell is very measured with his words, and rightfully so since just a few words from him can move markets in a big way. Because of this, I would take the term "fairly highly valued" as being a very tempered comment, which means to me that this should be read as a serious warning sign for investors. That's because a less measured person probably would be using more forceful language to describe the current valuation levels of the stock market.

Overall have been bullish for many years, but I have to agree with Mr. Powell that valuations appear too high, and I have become bearish on the market recently. I believe that the combination of historically high valuations for the stock market, coupled with signs that the economy is slowing, makes this a potentially treacherous time to be heavily invested in the market. Let's take a closer look below at some valuation metrics which appear to confirm that Jerome Powell is right when he suggests that the stock market is "fairly highly valued":

The Chart Suggests This Market Is "Fairly High Valued"

This chart shows the massive rebound from the April lows, (which were caused by Liberation Day tariff concerns), and that the market is now even higher than ever. From a purely technical viewpoint, this market appears very overbought and very extended beyond key support levels such as the 50-day moving average, which is around 640, and the 200-day moving average which is near 595. We all had a chance to buy at or near the April lows, so it is hard to understand getting excited about buying at or near all time highs. We are always hearing the adage "buy low, sell high," but right now it seems that many investors are hoping to buy high, and sell higher, which is a far more risky investment strategy. When I look at this chart, I see what could be a blow-off top, so I would not be surprised to see a major pullback fairly soon.

The Buffett Indicator Suggests This Market Is "Fairly Highly Valued"

Warren Buffett uses a valuation metric called the "Buffett Indicator," which takes the entire value of the U.S. stock market and divides it by the Gross Domestic Product or "GDP." The current estimated value of the U.S. stock market is near $65.47 trillion and the U.S. GDP is nearly $30 trillion. This indicates a 217% ratio of market value to GDP. By looking at the chart below, it is clear that the valuation of the U.S. stock market has surged in the past few years, and it might be at unsustainable levels.

The CAPE Ratio Suggests This Market Is "Fairly Highly Valued"

The cyclically adjusted price to earnings ratio or "CAPE" ratio is currently around 38. This is extraordinarily high since the CAPE ratio's historical average is around 17. This valuation metric was developed by Robert Shiller, a Nobel laureate and it is designed to be more sophisticated than the standard price to earnings ratio. The CAPE ratio uses the price of the S&P 500 Index and divides it by the average of the last 10 years of inflation-adjusted earnings. This smooths out the impact of short-term economic cycles which provides a clearer long-term valuation picture. When the CAPE ratio exceeds 30, it historically has been a sign of an overvalued market. It's worth noting that the CAPE ratio hit a high of about 44, in December 1999, and then the dot-com bubble crashed in 2000. With the CAPE ratio at 38 now, we don't have too much farther to go before it hits 44, but I would rather be raising cash a little before everyone else does.

The Price-To-Book Ratio Suggests This Market Is "Fairly Highly Valued"

This ratio compares a company's stock price to its book value per share. A high price-to-book ratio suggests the market is valuing a company well above the value of its tangible assets, so this is another popular valuation metric. The current price-to-book ratio is just over 5 times now, which is high compared to historical norms. In fact, by taking a look at the chart below, you can see that the last time the price-to-book ratio went well above 5, was back in 1999. This was the peak since the dot-com bubble crashed in 2000.

Where I Could Be Wrong

Maybe this time is different since we are in an unprecedented era with AI just about to change the world, just as the Internet started to do about 25 years ago (although that did not stop the market from plunging.) But, maybe this bubble could get bigger before things go bust. There is definitely a lot of momentum and that could take valuations higher. There is also a lot of cash on the sidelines which could lead to about $7.31 trillion being taken out of money market accounts as that money looks for a new home, since yields are expected to fall. If the Fed keeps cutting rates, this could also help fuel the stock market rally, and push valuations even higher.

In Summary

I think Jerome Powell was being very diplomatic to suggest that the market is "fairly highly valued." I also think it was notable that he even went out of his way to discuss stock market valuations, and perhaps it was meant as a subtle warning to anyone who wants to listen. Former Fed Chair, Alan Greenspan once described the stock market as having "irrational exuberance." This was back in December 1996, and the market bubble kept going for about 3 more years. However, multiple valuation metrics show we are at levels that warrant caution, so this is a time to become more defensive. Based on this, I plan to keep buying stocks when I see strategic opportunities, and I plan to keep core long-term positions. However, I have been raising cash overall and plan to continue doing so until a much better buying opportunity arises.

data.title: Devon Energy: Lower For Longer, Low Costs Will Deliver Gains
data.description:
Introduction

We have been following Devon Energy Corporation (NYSE:DVN) for a long time. It's an affair that's run hot and cold over the years, but back in early August it heated up again after its Q2 report. We put out a Buy note in the Daily Drilling Report on an undervaluation basis, citing the change in leadership and the company's commitment to generate a billion dollars in increased free cash flow by the end of next year through business optimization. These seemed like strong enough potential catalysts to us, we were ready to put some sidelined capital to work. We been gratified since with the quick 12% gain, and we are still bullish.

The entire big cap shale cohort has been kicked to the curb this year, and especially since Liberation Day. The sector has rebounded, but none has made it back to previous levels. Thanks to the recent rally from the low $30's, Devon is now less wounded than the others on a percentage basis, but still presents an attractive entry picture for investors in our view.

We see continued upside in this sector broadly and in Devon in particular. We will discuss our view on the catalysts we think will drive the company higher as 2025 closes out and the new year begins. I think investors looking for solid near term upside and capital returns a little farther out on the cash flow curve, should consider if upstream E&P's fit into their portfolio.

Let's talk about surviving and thriving in a low oil price world

If lower for longer-not something I think persists for much longer, but if...then grinding down F&D costs will be critical. Once again we turn to Rob Connor's "Crude Chronicles" work as an indicator of past success. Devon, always in the top 3-4 is in the #2 position, right behind Diamondback (FANG) in 2024. That's a good sign that through constantly pushing the goal post on D&C costs has not, and if commentary from management is to be believed, reached a point of diminishing returns. Low cost of supply is the long term determinant of winners and losers. It's good to see DVN is shaping up to be a winner.

As we discussed earlier, DVN has proactively identified internal levers from which they will extract a billion dollars of free cash by the end of 2026. A billion dollars in free cash is a lofty goal, and one it seems on which they have made a substantial start as this slide from their Q2 earnings call reveals.

If you add up the numbers in bold in the right hand column, you will see they total $400 mm, which is not too shabby a start. This could be low-hanging fruit, although I am gratified to see a quarter of it came from efficiency gains in drilling and completion.

Here Clay Gaspar, CEO comments on the internal assets used to realize these results:

By leveraging our existing -- or excuse me, our extensive data streams and our proprietary in-frac and in drill AI agents, we're able to capture operational enhancements in real time and drive efficiency in our critical operations. In parallel to this real-time operational assistance, we're also leveraging design improvements, simul-frac implementation and relentless focus on safety and execution. These enhancements have resulted in another 12% year-over-year improvement in drilling costs and a 15% improvement in completion costs. These are not just onetime gains.

Needless to say, completion costs have out-stripped drilling costs. I think the ratio in the case of intensive fracs may be 70/30 in favor of completion. In drilling, you are just grinding up the rock and pumping it out of the hole-no mean feat to be sure, and (in case any of you reading this are drillers, or have relatives who are, I mean no disrespect at all), but not as cash intensive as completions. Moving on.

The big ticket in cash flow enhancement seems to be the GP&T-Gathering, Processing, and Transportation costs for gas. Given their volumes, and the gas weighting of their output it looks this could continue to bear fruit.

The next critical deliverable is debt reduction. DVN owes a ton of money, although even at its elevated state of $8.4 bn, net debt to EBITDAX is a pretty solid multiple as noted in the slide below.

The company emphasized their commitment to getting down to a terminal, optimal debt load of around $6.0 bn by YE 2026.

We exited the quarter with $4.8 billion in total liquidity, including $1.8 billion in cash on hand. Our net debt-to-EBITDAX ratio improved to 0.9x, reflecting our ongoing focus on maintaining a strong balance sheet. Our $2.5 billion debt reduction plan is progressing well with $500 million already retired. Additionally, we plan to accelerate the retirement of our $485 million senior notes maturing in December.

Total capital guidance is being reduced by $100 million to a range of $3.6 billion to $3.8 billion. Importantly, DVN's breakeven funding level remains highly competitive at less than $45 WTI, including the dividend.

Catalysts for Devon

The One Big Beautiful Bill-Law. We haven't heard much about impact of this newly passed law discussed previously in such detail. DVN expects to save as much as a billion dollars in taxes over the next three years. In the quote below Jeff Ritenour, CFO made some bold predictions about the impact to free cash flow:

We now expect our full year 2025 current tax rate to be about -- to be around 10%, down from our previous estimate of 15%, adding nearly $300 million in projected cash flow for the year. As a result, we anticipate our ongoing current tax rate will be significantly lower than previous estimates, ranging between 5% and 10%. This reduction will provide Devon with increased cash flow of approximately $1 billion over the next 3 years, assuming a similar pricing environment and capital spend. This is in addition to the $1 billion of incremental free cash flow from our business optimization plan.

This is cash that can be used to accelerate debt paydown or fund capex to take advantage of price spikes.

Next Devon is doing a good job getting around the WAHA Hub choke point with future gas sales. Two new opportunities were highlighted in the call:

The first is a 10-year gas sales agreement to an LNG counterparty starting in 2028, under which Devon will sell 50 million cubic feet a day of natural gas at a Gulf Coast delivery point with pricing indexed to international markets. As LNG build-out creates additional demand for natural gas, they expect to pursue more opportunities to add exposure to international price markers.

The second is a Permian gas sales agreement with Competitive Power Ventures Basin Ranch Energy Center to support its proposed 1,350-megawatt power plant. With an expected start in 2028, Devon will supply 65 million cubic feet per day of natural gas for a 7- year term with pricing indexed to ERCOT West power prices. This pricing construct further limits Devon's exposure to the Waha price weakness we've seen in the basin for some time.

Risks

Further weakness in WTI is the key risk to shares. With realizations in the low $60's they managed to generate a lot of free cash in the quarter-$589 mm, but it's down 40% QoQ. Realizations in Q1 were in the $70's, easily explaining the drop, in spite of the cost cutting and revenue enhancements they are undertaking. Giving us some cushion at current prices is DVN's low EV/EBITDA multiple against competitors.

Your takeaway

DVN is doing a lot of right things-cutting costs to deliver the increased $1.0 bn in free cash by YE 2026. With 40% already structurally removed the progress is substantial and the target in sight and on time.

With debt at 1X EV/EBITDA their multiple should rerate a couple of points on that alone. With it's plans to retire $250-$300 mm quarterly in shares, we could see the share count in the 590-600 mm. With another $2.0 bn of free cash per year, FC/S should be in the $6.00-8.00 range. I'm being conservative here as DVN projects $3.0 bn for 2025 with $65.00 WTI. That's about $5.00 per share.

Without going crazy on napkin math, increased free cash should give the stock substantial upside pressure in the next year. There is an outlier estimate for the stock of $66.00. A multiple of 5.0X delivers that stock price easily.

DVN remains a Strong Buy for appreciation that is likely to come from increased cash flow. Catalysts are in place for this to occur at current WTI and gas prices. It should also be noted that with DVN's gas heavy production-about 26%, and increasing as the company begins to develop more Tier II and III reservoirs, that with NYMEX forecasts for this commodity in the next year, cash flow could see a continued boost.

data.title: Veradigm: A Relisting Special Situation With Strategic Optionality
data.description:
Background

To understand the opportunity, it helps to understand the wreckage. Veradigm (OTCMKTS: MDRX), known as Allscripts until 2022, has been around long enough to live through several cycles in healthcare IT. The company rebranded after selling its hospital and large physician EHR business to Constellation Software (TSE: CSU) for $700 million. That divestiture marked a turning point, rather than trying to compete head-on with Epic and Cerner in the large hospital market, a battle it was steadily losing, Veradigm leaned into the ambulatory side of the industry.

This pivot left it with a surprisingly durable niche. The ambulatory market is smaller, fragmented and in some ways defensible. Veradigm’s two main EHR offerings, Practice Fusion and Veradigm HER, serve smaller practices ranging from solo physicians up to 50 providers. These aren’t the mega systems that automatically roll into Epic or Oracle’s Cerner after a hospital acquisition. They are the long tail of outpatient medicine, often under pressure from reimbursement costs but also sticky once they adopt an EHR system.

These contracts are sticky because contracts are typically three to five years, and switching EHR’s is painful. Once a practice sets up billing, scheduling, and clinical workflows, ripping it for another system is costly, risky, and unnecessary. That explains why Veradigm’s provider segment, which makes up to 80% of the revenues, has historically grown in the low single digits without high churn.

The other 20% of the revenue comes from Payer & Life Sciences, where Veradigm monetizes its clinical and claims data. This is a higher margin faster growing business with products like risk adjustment tools for health plans, digital display ads embedded in Practice Fusion, and real-world evidence datasets sold to pharmaceutical companies. It’s not yet at the scale of peers like IQVIA (NYSE: IQV), but the growth trajectory has been attractive.

Taken together, this mix makes Veradigm a recurring revenue franchise with two very different profiles, a sticky slow-growing provider base and a smaller fast-growing data business. In normal times this was a reliable free cash flow story with a net cash balance sheet. Unfortunately, normal times ended in 2023.

Restatements, Delisting, and Self-Inflicted Wounds

The unraveling began in February 2023, when Veradigm disclosed accounting errors tied to a revenue recognition tool implemented in 2021. At first the company estimated $20 million cumulative impact across 2021-2022. That sounded manageable but the scope kept widening. By mid-2023, the restatement covered multiple years, management admitted that a new supplemental revenue system had to be built, and deadlines slipped from September to later in the year.

By December 2023, the board had ousted the CEO and the CFO. The company limped into 2024 without 10-Ks or 10-Qs, leaving investors in the dark. Nasdaq finally pulled the plug in February 2024, delisting the stock. That alone was a devastating blow. Many institutions cannot own OTC traded names and sell side coverage tends to vanish when a stock leaves the exchange.

Instead of tightening focus Veradigm baffled the investors by acquiring a pre revenue ScienceIO, a 22 person AI startup for $140 million. The optics were terrible. Here was a company that couldn’t file financials writing a nine-figure check for a pre revenue venture. Shareholders revolted and by mid-2024 the board was under immense pressure to “do something”.

That “something” was a strategic review. Over 30 potential acquirers signed NDAs, including Mckesson, Oracle, Thoma Bravo, CVS Health etc. Several submitted preliminary bids. Yet in early 2025 the process collapsed after no buyer was willing to close without audited financials. The stock sold off heavily on the news.

From an outsider’s perspective this was a case study in how not to manage a crisis. What could have been a one-to-two-year cleanup turned into multiyear odyssey of self-inflicted wounds. Still, the underlying business didn’t disappear. Customers stayed, cash balances held up, and the company continued to sign contracts.

Investment Thesis

At $4.70 a share Veradigm trades like a melting ice cube. The perception is a broken company destined to drift OTC indefinitely. But the numbers tell a different story, as of the first quarter 2025 Veradigm held $272 million in cash against the debt of $208 million leaving a modest net cash position of $0.59 per share. Preliminary revenue for the quarter came in between $145-$147 million essentially flat compared to the prior year, which suggests market is assigning an unusually low multiple to a sticky recurring revenue health care IT franchise. While not a growth story at the moment the stability is notable given the ongoing accounting overhang.

The disconnect between fundamentals and valuation is almost entirely sentiment driven. A messy restatement process, a failed strategic review, and the stigma of being delisted from Nasdaq have combined to make Veradigm uninvestable for many institutions. Yet these issues are temporary rather than structural. Management has guided to complete financial filings in 2025 with a goal of relisting on Nasdaq in 2026. If and when that happens Veradigm will regain credibility, access to a much broader pool of investors, and the possibility of renewed strategic interest from private equity buyers or industry consolidators.

Strategic Optionality

The other piece of the thesis is strategic value. Healthcare IT has been a fertile hunting ground for private equity in recent years. Consider a few deals:

  • NextGen Healthcare was acquired by Thoma Bravo in 2023 at roughly 15x EBITDA
  • R1 RCM was taken private by TowerBrook in 2024 at 14.3x EBITDA.
  • Athenahealth was bought by Veritas/Elliot in 2019 at 16x EBITDA.

In each case the thesis was the same recurring revenue, sticky contracts, and undermanaged costs. Buyers believed they could drive 200-500 bps of margin expansion through efficiencies.

Veradigm fits this template almost perfectly. Analysts estimate a buyer could extract $120 million in synergies, equal to 20% of the sales. That is a powerful lever. It explains why more than 30 parties looked at the company in the first process despite the lack of clean audits. The problem wasn’t lack of interest it was lack of audited financials.

Once Veradigm is relisted with audited numbers I see no reason why sponsors wouldn’t revisit it. In fact, the failed process arguably sets up a cleaner second attempt. Everyone knows interest is there, and the pool of potential buyers is broad.

Q1 2025 Preliminary Earnings Review

Despite all the noise Q1 showed the core business is functioning. Interim CEO Tom Langan highlighted $30 million in annual contract value, up 25% YoY. That’s not hypergrowth but it’s proof that customers aren’t walking away.

Cash burn was modest. The $23 million in cash reflected $13 million in acquisition payments, $6 million in capex, and $4 million in operating outflows. Importantly about $15 million for restatement and one-time costs weighed on the quarter. Without those cash flow would have looked better.

On the liability side Veradigm added a $100 million secured term loan in June 2025, drawing $75 million at closing. It carries SOFR + 750 bps, payable in cash or in kind through 2027. Crucially no warrants or equity were attached, which preserves shareholder value. By quarter end Q2, total debt is expected to be $283 million but net cash stays positive.

For the full year 2025, management reaffirmed revenue guidance of $583-588 million, essentially flat with 2024. Flat isn’t exciting but stability is valuable when sentiment is this broken.

Catalysts

The investment case rests on tangible milestones

  • Restatement Completion (2025): Removes the biggest overhang
  • Nasdaq Relisting (2026): Restores liquidity, reopens the stocks to wide investor base.
  • Strategic Optionality: Clean numbers make M&A viable again.
  • Margin Recovery: Margin upside as restatement costs roll off.
  • Capital Allocation: With net cash, buybacks are possible post relist.

Risks

No thesis is bulletproof. Here’s what keeps this one in check:

  • Execution Risk: Another delay in filings would severely test patience.
  • Operational Drift: Competitors like Athenahealth or NextGen could chip away while Veradigm is distracted.
  • Legal Exposure: Restatements often invite lawsuits
  • No Sale Outcome: That’s not disastrous as the relisting alone would be enough to reprice the stock substantially but it could limit upside.

Conclusion

Veradigm is a broken growth story turned into a special situation. The business is intact, customers are sticky, cash is real, and revenues are recurring. The problem has been governance and execution, not product or market.

For patient investors the opportunity is asymmetric. The downside is cushioned by a stable revenue base and a modest net cash position. On the upside, the most tangible catalyst is a rerating once Veradigm completes its filings and secures a Nasdaq relisting. Beyond that while not guaranteed the company could also regain strategic appeal either through renewed review process or unsolicited interest from private equity or industry consolidators.

This isn’t a bet on AI hype or aggressive growth, it’s a bet on the process, fix the filings, regain a listing, and let the business be valued for what it is. In my view that’s enough to make $MDRX one of the more interesting, relisting special situations in the market today.

data.title: Okta: Brace For Disappointment Against Overly Optimistic Estimates
data.description:
Okta (NASDAQ:OKTA) has been building on its operating leverage progress, leading to a second straight quarter of positive GAAP operating income. The cybersecurity company remains as relevant as ever, even if I expect growth rates to be meaningfully below consensus estimates. The net cash balance sheet should not be underestimated, but I question whether the profit margins are high enough to justify even its conservative valuation. I continue to recommend avoiding the stock.

OKTA Stock Price

I last covered OKTA in June, where I continued to recommend avoiding the stock, with management appearing to signal decelerating growth ahead. The stock has fallen around 9% since.

This past quarter appears to be showing more evidence that the large beat and raises of the past are over.

OKTA Stock Key Metrics

OKTA is an enterprise software company with a focus on securing identity. You may have come across the product or a similar kind of product, as they help secure access to applications using two-factor authentication.

The main elephant in the room has been competition from the cloud titan Microsoft (MSFT). OKTA believes that it offers a superior product, and there may be some truth to that given the company’s ability to sustain double-digit growth at scale.

The most recent quarter saw the company generate 13% YoY revenue growth to $728 million, narrowly beating guidance of $712 million. That is important to note given the company had delivered much bigger beats in many of the past quarters.

The company did see its dollar-based net retention rate remain stable sequentially for the first time since last year.

The company also delivered a beat on profitability, with non-GAAP operating income coming in at $202 million, beating guidance of $185 million and representing nearly 500 bps of margin expansion. The company also delivered a positive GAAP operating income for the second straight quarter. I expect the company to sustain GAAP profitability moving forward.

OKTA ended the quarter with $2.9 billion of cash versus $859 million of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet. The company has not been repurchasing shares, but I expect a share repurchase program to be authorized some time over the next couple of quarters.

Looking ahead, management guided for the third quarter to see up to 10% YoY revenue growth and 22% non-GAAP operating margins. I expect both of these to prove conservative, especially on profitability.

Management raised full-year expectations to see up to 11% YoY revenue growth to $2.885 billion, up from the prior guide of $2.86 billion. That implies around $739 million in fourth-quarter revenue, representing just 8.4% YoY growth exiting the year.

On the conference call, management again noted that their guidance should not be considered conservative. Management noted that this guidance no longer has the “layer of prudence for macro” that they had previously embedded. Management noted that they are “trying to shoot for closer to the pin than we have historically.” I do not see any reason for management to be repeatedly stressing this point other than to be preparing investors for an eventual deceleration in growth rates.

Is OKTA Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, OKTA traded hands at around 5.5x sales. Consensus estimates call for high-single-digit top-line growth moving forward.

Based on my projection for 30% net margins over the long term, that places the stock right around 18x long-term earnings. I had previously noted that I’d be more interested at 5x sales, or 16.7x long-term earnings. The stock does not trade far from that, and I note that consensus estimates even for several years out are above the 8.4% YoY growth rate implied for the fourth quarter exiting the year. Consensus estimates look too aggressive here, and OKTA has not made enough progress on profitability to back up the valuation through things like meaningful share repurchases. If OKTA had a more significant GAAP operating margin, then I’d be more inclined to rate the stock a "Buy" here given the long-term growth (albeit lower than consensus) and net cash balance sheet. Perhaps one might like OKTA as a potential takeover candidate, but I am unconvinced that such a thesis would consistently work well from these valuations.

OKTA Stock Conclusion

OKTA might not be as expensive as many software peers, and indeed, it trades in the lower to middle end of software stocks in my coverage universe. The company has also been making meaningful progress on driving GAAP profitability while maintaining a net cash balance sheet. The profit margins are, however, not high enough to justify the valuation in light of what I believe to be a wave of disappointment against consensus estimates. I recommend investors look elsewhere for quality names, as I have done for the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Neutral" rating for the stock.

data.title: Valeura: The Market Continues To Ignore Every Positive Development
data.description:
Valeura Energy Inc. (OTCQX:VLERF) is a small-cap Toronto-listed Oil company operating exclusively in the Gulf of Thailand. As noted in my prior coverage, the company is exceedingly cheap, a point I will reinforce here given recent developments. Any investor buying today is likely to see all the money come back in free cash flow within the next 24-30 months (at current lower oil prices).

At current prices ($70/Bbl Brent) the company will generate around $130 million in free cash per year. The $587 million market cap is supported by $242 million in cash-on-hand (40% of market cap) and there is no debt. The result is an enterprise value of only $345 million. Should Brent go to $85/Bbl the purchase price would be recovered in full after only 18 months.

There is of course a risk that the company experiences lower oil prices for a prolonged period of time – but the huge cash cushion ensures such a risk does not turn into an “existential” one.

The issue that the market seems to focus on and gets people to misunderstand the company is the very low reported reserve life. Taken at face value, the valuation does not look cheap, as reserves would run out in the early 2030s (accelerating asset-retirement obligations as well). However, we cannot take the reserves at face value, as the company (and the region) has consistently extended reserves through in-fill drilling.

July's agreement with PTTEP is the most recent example of the company doing all it can to show investors there is a long way to go here – and yet, after an initial “pop” in the stock price, the shares have drifted back to the pre-announcement levels.

The most critical factor for this thesis is to understand the gap that exists between the reported reserve life and the actual experience of reserve performance. Stated another way, the market views the current production rate of 20-25 kbbls/d as ending in four to six years—while the reality is that the rate can be sustained for 20 years or more—and perhaps grow somewhat as well. The issue is crucial to understanding the valuation gap and is covered more fully in this article.

Both the Wassana redevelopment and the PTTEP farm-in are rock-solid “evidence” that the cash flows we see today (and that we can buy at less than 3 years' worth) are sustainable well into the 2040s.

The Wassana Redevelopment:

The project consists of $120 million investment to replace the current MOPU with a Central Processing Platform (CPP) which will increase current field production from 3.4 kbbls/d to 10 kbbls/d.

The economics are very solid – to say the least:

Crucially, when completed in 2027, the project will extend Wassana reserve life beyond 2040. A fact that should bring investors around to understanding the durability of cash flows.

As things stand, without considering the farm-in (to be discussed next), all the fields operated by Valeura have extended their remaining lives after every year-end assessment – demonstrating how infill drilling and upgrading infrastructure are allowing each field to earn a “new lease" on life:

Given the results of the latest drilling program (summarized in the article linked in the previous section), we are almost certain to see this trend toward extended lives continue for the 2025 report. And then there is the farm-in agreement.

The Farm-in agreement:

In late July, Valeura announced another transaction aimed at extending their operations for decades, not years. They are teaming up with the largest hydrocarbon producer in the Thai Gulf, state-owned PTTEP for developing two blocks G1/65 and G3/65.

The new blocks to be developed have promising prospects and are adjacent to gas-producing infrastructure owned by the partner and also to some of Valeura’s oil platforms.

More importantly, they are huge. They do a 9x on Valeura’s working acreage in the region. Their involvement is financial in nature; the company will retain a 40% non-operating interest in future developments there.

The price is (as in all prior transactions) very rational. Valeura will pay its share of past exploration expenses to PTTEP and will also carry the full cost of 3D seismic work along an area of special interest to Valeura – given its proximity to producing assets. The total cost is expected to be less than $20 million, barely making a dent in their cash flow pile.

What these two developments mean:

Both the Wassana redevelopment and the PTTEP farm-in are clear signs that investors can expect to see cash flows well into the 2040s. We are already there with Wassana - and future fields from the new blocks will only add length to that view.

For investors worried that oil has a finite role in energy markets—which I don’t see coming within any relevant horizon – but recognized others might. Most of the production from PTTEP-operated blocks is likely to be gas. Just look at the fields immediately to the West of the yellow acreage (red fields mean gas fields):

Mostly gas will likely be produced within those blocks, helping Valeura diversify revenues away from oil-only. At the same time, gas demand within Thailand is sure to grow, ensuring a ready consumer market at the nearest shores.

Risks:

Obviously, lower oil prices will affect cash flows. This risk is mitigated by both the lack of debt and the very large cash balance. The company can certainly operate at lower than $60 Brent and will further lower their break-even levels in 2027 with COD of the Wassana platform.

Although all production is “offshore”, the gulf is relatively shallow and therefore the drilling and undersea works are not complicated or expensive. The risk of Hurricanes (or severe winds) is diminished by the geography but juxtaposed with the risk of earthquakes.

Thailand is an investment-grade country with a long tradition of honoring contracts in the oil and gas industry. Chevron is a third large player in the region; and it is always good to have at least one "major" around. All current licenses have renewal options in favor of the operator for an additional 10 years, the first of which is being processed right now. Traditionally concessions for extensions have been reasonable, we shall see how Valeura is treated in its first attempt.

With the PTTEP farm-in and the Wassana expansion I don’t see the production volume risk as relevant as it was before. But current production needs successful in-fill drilling programs. If they hit a dry spell on infills at some of their fields, production could trend lower at least for a while, until the new investments start to bear fruit (2027 for Wassana and beyond that for the farm-in).

Finally, operational risks are always a concern with O&G upstream. Valeura, although new as an owner, has retained most of the employees from the two acquired concerns (KrisEnergy and Mubadala). These operators are experienced in the specific geology and conditions of the Gulf.

As for the construction of the CPP to go at Wassana, the design is a copy of an already operational CPP at Nong Yao, the EPC contract is mostly locked-in with regard to price, and the construction period is not that long (24 months). Cost overruns or significant delays are unlikely given the quality of the contractor which is Nippon Steel Engineering and Construction.

Valuation:

The company makes a pretty good case for what it thinks about their own valuation:

As cheap as the company looks on cash flow metrics above—which imply it is trading at 1/2 to 1/3 of the peer median—all of these disregard the potential for selling down or developing the company's very large gas prospect in Turkey (estimated at 20 Tcfe for Valeura's working interest of 82%).

Very little progress has been reported on this front but eventually some way to unlock value will come into view (likely a farm-out as implied by management comments). Regardless of how this comes to pass, the gas is there, and Europe surely will need it. This "hidden asset" provides us with an even wider margin of safety than implied by multiples above.

Takeaway:

The asymmetric tradeoff on Valeura is compelling and rare. The oversized cash position shields investors from exposure to a prolonged drawdown in Brent. Such a drawdown usually creates ample opportunity for cash-rich operators (which is how Valeura came into the Gulf of Thailand in 2020-2021) and the usual price spike on the other side.

The valuation seems unfathomably low and can only be explained by a misunderstanding of the true nature and longevity of the cash flows concerned. The Wassana redevelopment FID and the farm-in agreement with PTTEP are both concrete and recent examples of the long-term potential—yet the market continues to ignore these developments… It won't do that forever - Strong Buy.
