data.title: Keyera: Benefitting From Basin Growth
data.description:
Keyera Corp. (TSX:KEY:CA) wants to be the North American leader in delivering energy infrastructure solutions. I generally like energy infrastructure businesses because they often have high barrier to entry assets.

Company background

Keyera is based in Calgary, Canada. It is the smallest of the four main Canadian Oil and Gas Storage and Transportation stocks, the others being Enbridge Inc (ENB, ENB:CA), Pembina Pipeline Corp (PBA, PPL:CA), and TC Energy Corp (TRP, TRP:CA).

The company operates a fully integrated value chain centered around transporting, processing, and marketing Natural Gas Liquids, NGLs.

Keyera has three operating segments: Gathering and Processing; Liquids Infrastructure; and Marketing. In financial reporting, Keyera often groups Gathering and Processing and Liquids Infrastructure together under the name Fee-for-Service Segments. In the last three years, realized margin (which is operating margin excluding unrealized gains and losses from commodity related risk management contracts) averaged 66% for Fee-for-Service and 34% for Marketing, as a percent of total realized margin.

The fee-for-service is renting volume to third parties for its storage and transportation assets, while the marking business "markets a range of products associated with its two infrastructure business lines, primarily propane, butane, condensate and iso-octane, and also engages in liquids blending activities." In other words marketing expose Keyera to commodity market price risks, which it looks to manage.

A detailed description of the business can be found on its Annual Information Form, year ending December 31, 2024.

The following map shows Keyera’s principal operations in Canada.

Financial analysis and critical factors

Keyera, Pembina and TC Energy all trade with a trailing P/E of around 20x. Enbridge which is one of the world's largest energy companies, has a trailing P/E of 24x, I believe because of a more attractive growth outlook, which was set out in its 2025 Investor Day presentation.

Keyera's last Investor Day was in 2022, so I'll focus on more recent data, but the Investor Day presentation did highlight a couple of things I will come back to: KAPS, which it called "A Game Changer" and a Strategic Relationship With Shell.

Normally when I look at a new company, in the U.S. I will go to the latest 10-K, and in Europe, I will go to the Annual Report. In Canada there is no one document. Instead we must go to the Annual Information Form, audited Annual Financial Statements, and the Management Discussion and Analysis. At Keyera, the MD&A and financial statements can be found in its Year-End Report.

In terms of trying to understand the critical factors that will drive Keyera's earnings, let's take a look at the assets and metrics of each segment that are going to dictate financial performance.

Gathering and Processing

According to the 2024 Management’s Discussion and Analysis:

Keyera currently has interests in 9 active gas plants, all of which are located in Alberta. Keyera operates 7 of the 9 active gas plants. The Gathering and Processing segment includes raw gas gathering systems and processing plants strategically located in the natural gas production areas on the western side of the Western Canada Sedimentary Basin (“WCSB”).

Net processing throughput measured in million standard cubic feet per day, or MMcf/d, which refers to Keyera’s share of raw gas processed at its processing facilities, is the key metric here. In 2024 this metric was down 3% over 2023. However, in the first half of of 2025, this metric was up 7% vs the same period in 2024. Likely to a major extent the underlying exposure is natural gas production on the western side of the Western Canada Sedimentary Basin.

Liquids Infrastructure

According to the 2024 Management’s Discussion and Analysis:

The Liquids Infrastructure segment provides fractionation, storage, transportation, liquids blending and terminaling services for NGLs and crude oil, and produces iso-octane. These services are provided to customers through an extensive network of facilities, including the following assets:

  • NGL and condensate pipelines
  • Underground NGL storage caverns
  • Above ground storage tanks
  • NGL fractionation and de-ethanization facilities
  • Pipeline, rail and truck terminals
  • Liquids blending facilities
  • The AEF facility (Alberta EnviroFuels).

Net processing throughput, measured in thousand barrels per day, or Mbbl/d, is the key measure here, along with the AEF iso-octane production volumes also measured in Mbbl/d. In 2024 net processing throughput in the Liquids Infrastructure segment was down 4% over 2023 and AEF iso-octane production volumes were down 13% in 2024 vs 2023. In the first half of 2025, net processing throughput was up 4% vs H1 2024, while AEF iso-octane production volumes were down 17% period on period.

NGLs, condensate and iso-octane have various demand drivers including oil sands diluent, gasoline blending, petrochemicals, residential heating, so it is difficult to narrow this segment down to one core demand driver, while the supply driver is coming from condensate and NGL production levels.

Capacity utilization of transportation and storage assets would be an important metric, but as yet I could not find detailed information on this, reported by the company.

Take-or-Pay

In the company's Fee-for-Service business, in 2024 52% of the realized margin, came from Take-or-Pay contracts. Take-or-Pay provides financial protection when a customer no longer needs booked capacity.

Marketing

Marketing is largely a function of sales volumes measured in barrels per day, or Bbl/d, and the spread between purchase and sales prices. This is essentially an arbitrage business exploiting price differentials between point A and point B, or between time A and time B, utilizing storage. Keyera also engages in blending products of lower value into higher value products. Marketing is benefits from integration with Keyera's Gathering and Processing and Liquids Infrastructure assets (hence the inter-segment eliminations).

The 2024 Management’s Discussion and Analysis also notes:

In a typical year, Keyera expects the Marketing business to contribute on average, a base realized margin of between $310 million and $350 million. This guidance assumes: i) a crude oil price of between US$65 and US$75 per barrel; ii) butane feedstock costs comparable to the 10-year average; and iii) AEF utilization at nameplate capacity.

With good risk management, Keyera would benefit from volatile markets.

Growth opportunities

The macro backdrop is a bit mixed: Power generation and industrial use should support natural gas, I expect petrochemical demand growth too. Gasoline blending and oil sands may face longer term headwinds. However NGLs are low in carbon content, so they will be used to bring the carbon intensity down of other oil products.

However, with Canadian West Coast LNG exports set to ramp, with LNG Canada online, and Woodfibre LNG and LNG Canada Phase II announced and proposed proposed respectively, it would not surprise me if natural gas production in the Western Canada Sedimentary Basin were to ramp up to support gas flows for export.

Specifically, Keyera is expected to benefit from condensate and NGL production growth from the Montney and Duvernay basins in Western Canada.

The Key Access Pipeline System known as KAPS is the largest project in Keyera’s history. Completed in 2023 it is 50/50 joint venture with Stonepeak, which is "a leading alternative investment firm specializing in essential infrastructure." Keyera operates the pipeline, and an expansion project known as KAPS Zone 4 has been sanctioned and expected to come online mid-2027.

Another interesting project, signed in 2022 is the strategic relationship with Shell, looking to build a gathering and distribution network to transport captured CO2 from Keyera and other operations in Alberta’s "Industrial Heartland" to Shell Canada’s Atlas Carbon Storage Hub. In June 2024, Shell announced Final Investment Decision to proceed with the Atlas Carbon Storage Hub.

Acquisition of Plains’ Canadian NGL Business

More recently, Keyera announced the "Transformative" acquisition of Plains' Canadian NGL business. This will go into the Liquids Infrastructure business. Seeking Alpha author Trapping Value called this a Gift and a game changer - so I won't cover the deal in detail. The deal is expected to close in Q1 2026, subject to regulatory approvals.

However I do want to share a nice macro chart from its deal presentation.

Risks

So far so good. Keyera is another way to play the natural gas story and there appears to be volume growth, so maybe its P/E can rerate from 20x to closer to Enbridge's 24x.

So what challenges could the company face?

First its supply of diluent and solvents Canada's Oil Sands industry, could be problematic. Oil Sands are very high cost production, and if crude prices are not high, Oil Sands quickly become unprofitable which in theory would reduce production. This could negatively impact Keyera's Liquids Infrastructure segment.

As the company states in the 2024 Notes to annual consolidated financial statements:

Most of Keyera’s Liquids Infrastructure assets are located in, or connected to, the Edmonton/Fort Saskatchewan area in Alberta, one of four key energy hubs in North America. This area also serves as a condensate hub which supports the operations of customers in the oil sands sector. Condensate is used as a diluent to facilitate movement of bitumen by pipeline.

Then there is its Iso-octane business. This goes into motor gasoline, or more specifically Reformulated Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending or “RBOB.” If the Canadian government moves against internal combustion engine, then the demand for Iso-octane will fall.

Then there is the commodity price risk that can be seen in the spread between reported Operating margin and Realized margin. The difference is "unrealized loss on risk management contracts". In 2024, this spread appeared to be small, but it just takes one rogue trader to create large losses.

In terms of financial risk, it's Altman Z-Score is just 1.63 which is quite weak.

Old economy

Keyera's stock on the TSX is up 131% over the last five years. Not bad. Most of the assets of the company are coming from Property, plant and equipment. Modern value investors are looking at both tangible and intangible assets. So does Keyera have any intangible value?

For a start there is the high barriers to entry in the physical infrastructure system it has built. This is the main point.

What about technological developments and digital transformation? In its 2024 Annual Information Form, Keyera discusses this as a risk more than an opportunity. It says failure to stay on top of technological developments and digital transformation could "materially adversely affect Keyera’s operations and its ability to compete in the market." I can give credit to Keyera for not AI-washing its presentations. But it could be an opportunity as AI start-ups like Applied Computing bring innovation to the energy industry, especially the processing heavy. But for now, Keyera's main and important intangible comes from its attractive geographic positioning.

Valuation

The 20x trailing P/E is historically not cheap or expensive. But with the growth opportunities on the table, if Keyera can execute, it could rerate several points higher.

Conclusion

Keyera's guidance of Fee-Based Adjusted EBITDA CAGR of 7-8% from 2024 through 2027 seems easily doable. With a supportive macro backdrop and what looks to be a robust business the stock looks to be worth owning, as I look to add more natural gas or natural gas related exposure to my portfolio. Now I have introduced myself to the company, I know what to watch for and I'll be watching this stock closely.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Power Solutions International: A Data Center Rerating That Looks Overdone
data.description:
Power Solutions International, Inc. (NASDAQ:PSIX) is a classic overdone rerating story. The business has successfully pivoted to AI infrastructure and data center power systems. The fundamentals have improved and a visible turnaround in financials does deserve an upgrade. But there are still several risks on the table that are being overlooked in a classic momentum-driven trade, as PSIX rallies to new highs every day. The overall case for PSIX is that of a good business, not necessarily a good investment at these prices.

The Pivot: Industrial Engines to Data Center

PSIX is a traditional industrial designer and manufacturer of emission-tested engines and power systems. Its client base spans across industrials, power systems and transportation. PSIX has a significant track record of validated product fit, with its fuel-agnostic engines going through years of manufacturing, improvements, and sales. The strategic pivot that has caught the attention of the market is the power systems segment that has now seen traction in data center's power generation use cases.

Fundamental Indicators Solid Only on Surface

Let us look at some narrative-altering indicators and dig a little deeper into what they signal. Revenues have seemingly seen a breakout quarter in Q2 2025, with a ~74% YoY jump in topline. This is yet to be a story of sustained high growth, because if you remove the last quarter's uptick in revenues, the story is not one of consistent growth over time.

Q2 presents a similar breakthrough story for net income, up ~138% YoY. Again, a clear breakthrough pattern, but not backed by consistent performance across a few quarters. In fact, double-clicking on the income improvement reveals a close to $29.2m net income increase attributable to the release of valuation allowance on deferred tax assets, almost the entire increase in net income. This is not a breakthrough earnings quarter but rather a one-time accounting adjustment. The best news that can be derived from this is the resolution of going concern doubts and progress on debt management—notably a refinancing extending debt maturity to 2027.

Another factor that should keep the excitement in check is the explosion in SG&A by ~269% YoY to fund the revenue expansion. This is not great for PSIX's immediate operating leverage and also shows the revenue increase has not been simply an unaided demand uptick but more concerted growth efforts in sales and marketing—expected in the growth phase but puts the revenue explosion in better perspective.

Gross margins have also marginally contracted in Q2 through the scaling phase. While it's still the initial days of the pivot to AI and data center-led demand, gross margin pressures surely signal that there are several validations pending—notably how well PSIX does in the face of competition and its impact on pricing power.

Overall, the only silver lining in Q2 beyond headline income improvements is the quality of the revenue mix. While legacy demand remained stagnant, the power systems segment drove the entire revenue uptick, attributable to the data center use case. This is definitely an encouraging development and a solid rerating catalyst, but there are many questions still unanswered, primarily around sustained demand signals beyond just a quarter.

Valuations Stretched for Stage

PSIX's EV to forward Sales ratio of ~4.3x is extremely high for an industrials small cap. It makes the expectations of continued and uninterrupted hypergrowth too high and is built into share prices today. Where the reality is that apart from an encouraging foray into AI and data center demand, there are still many unanswered questions. Competitive and technology risks, or even pricing power remain open questions that are yet to be validated. A far bigger and more diversified player like Vertiv (with almost 10x revenue scale) commands a ~5.5x EV to forward Sales ratio. Vertiv's positioning is that of a far more mature business with lower execution risks and a somewhat proven competitive positioning. Of course, PSIX's valuations, if the hypergrowth continues, could still be justified. A slightly lower valuation with a superior topline growth profile should be palatable, but I will need to see that hypergrowth translating at the expected pace and without damaging other open questions like gross margins to become a believer.

To understand the valuations better, it is important to understand PSIX's competitive positioning. PSIX finds itself in a fragmented but highly competitive space. It does have some moat-like defense characteristics as seen in its fuel-agnostic tech, emission-friendly products and a customizable solution that can cater to specific client needs.

However, when it comes to the data center power markets, there is intense competition from bigger players who have already made significant inroads. Several research studies indicate varying numbers of market shares of the leaders in thermal management providers for data centers. Most of them definitely agree that Vertiv, Schneider and Daikin (and a few others) are market leaders in the segment. Even dated data on the data center cooling market in 2019-20 shows Vertiv as commanding ~23.5% of the total market. Which means the combined leadership of the top players accounts for the vast majority of the market and additionally has years of relationships and proven track records at scale. That not only highlights the magnitude of the challenge that PSIX faces to make continued inroads against established incumbents but also shows why PSIX's current valuations do not account for the difference in scale and execution risks compared to Vertiv.

DCF Model Shows PSIX is Overpriced

The valuations are stretched but still palatable under certain assumptions like a continued hypergrowth and a narrative premium due to AI and data center demand. But any hopes of even a Hold thesis are quashed by my conservative DCF model.

I have built the DCF model on a 5-year horizon keeping the tech and execution risks in mind. PSIX is still a small-cap, potentially rapid growth company in a fast-evolving sector—longer forecast periods will carry too much uncertainty. A realistic growth trajectory starting with a 30% topline growth in Year 1 and slowing down to around say 8% in 5 years (at sizeable revenue levels of ~$1.3b annually) is assumed. This growth profile respects the realities of competitive pressures and moderating growth on a larger base later on.

The FCF margins assumption acknowledges reduction in R&D and SG&A spends only over time as PSIX scales. A higher WACC of ~14% is taken to reflect small-cap risk premium and execution risks. This realistic model, which I would say is not overly pessimistic, shows an intrinsic value of $56 for PSIX, far below the current levels of well beyond $100.

I reverse checked what scenario is built into today's share prices of around $110. The current prices expect a ~70% growth in Year 1 (in line with the Q2 YoY growth seen). The topline growth then slows to ~40-50% in Years 2-3 and 25-30% in the last 2 years. This is almost an ask that ignores the lumpy nature of hyperscaler contracts and sales cycles and expects continued top-class execution amidst low competition. The FCF assumptions for today's prices should be ~16% margins in Year 1 and expanding to ~21% in Year 5, which assumes huge efficiencies right from the start. Even the terminal growth rate should be ~3% (instead of 2% in our realistic model) and a discount rate of ~12%. That is valuing PSIX almost like a less risky mid-cap. That is also a very high bar!

Management Guidance and Reality Check

Management commentary in Q2 2025 is optimistic on continued inroads in data center products, while the traditional business is expected to remain flat through 2025. There are no specific guidance targets on the growth levels as management quoted geopolitical uncertainties and tariff impacts as areas of caution.

Alongside greater macroeconomic uncertainties, there are other execution risks that the management is focusing on—notably streamlining manufacturing efforts and scaling manufacturing capacity to meet the increase in demand.

But Can't Sell

Having seen the peer valuations and DCF assumptions, the prices are looking inflated, but not as inflated as in some other narrative-based stocks in today's AI and data center-led optimism. Looking through the headline numbers, all is not rosy or proven yet, but the core story of expansion into AI and data centers is valid and worth the rerating. I feel data points to an overdone rerating and PSIX being a candidate for a Sell. But the gap between intrinsic value and share prices can be bridged with a couple of quarters of continued hypergrowth. Shorting PSIX, although quantitatively indicated, is not practically recommended.

I would at best encourage buying risk-contained put positions far dated and out of the money as a speculative call for action from the almost bearish thesis. But a Sell rating looks too bold, particularly given the fact that the narrative is real and several other stocks have ridden the momentum to far greater disparities from fundamentally indicated levels. So, I remain bearish on PSIX, rate it a Hold, and wait for any correction below at least ~$80 to be interested in longs.

data.title: Hershey: Premium Valuation Despite Significant Macro Issues, But Worth Watching
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

The Hershey Company (NYSE:HSY) is the #1 US confectionery manufacturer, with an expanding presence in non-chocolate snacks and some of the most popular sweets brands on the planet, including Reese's, Payday, Almond Joy, Kit Kat (US only), and of course, their flagship brand, Hershey's. Right now, the company is navigating through some tough times for the broader food industry, but some significant catalysts are developing, while their new CEO could offer potential for an accelerated recovery.

Their latest reports have been marked by a very significant drop in net income due to higher cost of sales (will mention below), while the cash from operations also dropped, especially due to working capital changes. Although these are very easily explained by macro changes, this created very significant volatility in the stock price over the past year, although they have recovered and now trade at basically the same price as a year ago. They're also expecting a drop in CAPEX to ~$425 million to $450 million, which is about $150 million to $175 million less than a year ago.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see that they are in an okay position, with the current assets well above the current liabilities and quite a bit of long-term debt. We see a ~$2 billion increase in long-term debt, although note that the short-term debt decreased by ~$1.14 billion as a result of the company issuing $2 billion back in February to refinance all of its outstanding 0.9% Senior Notes due 2025 and 3.2% Senior Notes due 2025. These are spread into four tranches: one in 2028 (4.55% interest rate), 2030 (4.75%), 2032 (4.95%), and 2035 (5.1%). This should add quite a bit of interest (about $76.25 million), which is definitely not a small amount given their cash flow, making them increase their interest expense guidance this year, as can be seen below.

Now, they expect $170 million to $180 million in tariff expenses combined with a massive drop in EPS of ~50% due to a combination of derivative mark-to-market gain back in 2024's reported earnings, a non-cash gain that won't repeat, combined of course with the price of cocoa even tripling due to West Africa's poor harvest levels and tariffs. I don't believe these are permanent issues, but it's also worth noting that the company's stock is still performing very well compared to peers, despite being affected by the same industry headwinds - or even more due to their dependency on chocolate.

Dividends

The company's dividend isn't really amazing despite the recent increase in yield, representing about $1.11 billion, which is a lot for the company in its current state. This shouldn't be an issue now, but if the macro uncertainty mentioned below continues to hurt their bottom line and volumes, this might have to be cut - and it's not like it was a significant yield anyway.

Risks & Opportunities

A point mentioned in previous articles is that the industry's (and consequently the company's) growth ultimately depends on the economic cycle and consumers' discretionary budgets. Virtually every company that depended on the consumer - such as Nike (NKE), PepsiCo (PEP), and many others - has reported issues in consumer demand, especially in North America and China, where most of them reported flat or even declining sales. In Hershey's case, this hasn't been a huge negative so far, as the company still managed to grow its revenue a bit and remain at relatively flat levels in terms of cash flow, but it definitely had an impact.

A major improvement that I believe should be their number one opportunity in the near term will be the effect of rate cuts, with two more cuts expected this year after the one in September. By depending on the discretionary budgets, and since they've been affected by a weaker consumer environment, I believe rate cuts could play an important role in a recovery when it comes to discretionary demand.

By lowering interest rates, the Fed basically increases discretionary budgets, which could be great for the company's consumers and the company itself, as that would lower interest rate payments for both sides. This can be great for HSY as well, since their interest expenses got to nearly $200 million over the past 12 months. This isn't really a debt-ridden company, but it can help regardless, especially since it would allow them to expand at lower rates in case they want to.

With their nearly 3% yield, it's also worth noting that rate cuts will be great for dividend stocks as well. Rate cuts have the effect of lowering treasury yields and make these stocks more attractive, as that money would seek better yields, potentially outside of the US as well. As mentioned before, I believe high rates and the resulting high treasury yields were a very significant factor when it comes to the overall weak performance of food stocks since the pandemic.

Another thing I want to note is that Hershey's products aren't really the most defensive. The same thing I said about Nestlé (OTCPK:NSRGY) in the recent article: when people have to tighten the belt, sweets should be one of the first steps they take. Somewhat related, another thing that's been visible for many industries is a de-premiumization effect due to lower discretionary budgets or higher prices (both, in this case), which is visible in these companies' volumes. We've seen this in recent years as many companies in the industry started reporting issues, including General Mills (GIS), which I analyzed recently and rated a Buy thanks to their overall high recovery potential.

On top of that, Hershey can be significantly affected by the current administration's fight against the industry, especially the products full of sugar and others. As HSY focuses on sweets, this impact can be significantly worse than it was for other players that I covered before, such as General Mills or Nestlé. They are focusing on better-for-you brands, but that doesn't mean there won't be any impact from current or especially future Make America Healthy Again policies. It remains an uncertainty to watch for now.

Trump's tariffs will also continue to play a very important role in the near future, and we've seen HSY already fall over 8% back when they trimmed the FY 2026 guidance and cocoa futures jumped 11%. Given the unpredictable nature of tariffs, simply increasing the costs to try and mitigate these is not necessarily the best idea (most companies did/will do this, including Hershey), as it can lead to worse volumes and still hurt the company's bottom line, while taking the hit and waiting for commodity prices to normalize can be a risky gamble, so they are caught in a tough spot. Regarding tariffs, this was their former CEO's response during the latest Q&A (this was before the 11% jump):

As we got hit by some of the record high cocoa prices early on, we stated that our approach was going to be taking a long-term approach to ensure continued category health, and we've done that. We've continued to spend on our brands, we've invested in technology with our new ERP platform, and then new AI and tech-enabled capabilities that have driven significant efficiency, whether in the transformation program or other places.

As a note, the company has had a new CEO since August, with a relatively good history as the CEO of Wendy's (WEN) and over 30 years of work at PepsiCo. He seems to be on the same track as the previous CEO, saying that he's committed to continuing the company's vision to be a major player in the broader snacking category, building on what the previous CEO started. This includes their expansion into non-chocolate snacks and managing the current industry challenges while also potentially focusing more on global expansion, which is an opportunity for Hershey as they are quite weak outside of North America.

Still, we are likely to see them keep trying to expand, which I think is a good move given their stock's valuation compared to peers. In fact, we've seen HSY expand through acquisitions like LesserEvil (announced in April 2025; ~$750 million, better-for-you snacks), Actual Candy in 2024, two facilities from Weaver Popcorn Manufacturing Facilities in 2023, and more.

However, Hershey itself could also be the target of an acquisition, with Mondelez (MDLZ) trying to acquire HSY last year, but the latter refused the bid, saying it was too low. This wasn't even their first attempt, as it was also tried before back in 2016. I believe such a deal (which would be breaking records given the size) could make sense for a more diversified player like Mondelez or PepsiCo, which would gain significant US exposure and be able to withstand industry headwinds such as the volatile cocoa prices, which is tougher for Hershey alone. Not only that, but such a giant could expand Hershey's brands internationally, benefitting from their massive logistics networks, which Hershey lacks internationally.

But another thing to note here is that the Hershey Trust owns most of the company's voting power through Class B shares, with about 78% of voting power based on their latest AGM. And even if the Trust would approve it, the regulatory uncertainty behind such a massive deal would remain tough.

Peer Comparison

However, when we get to the peer comparison, this is when HSY starts to look weak. Comparing them to local and global competitors such as Kellanova (K), General Mills, Nestlé, Mondelez, Chocoladefabriken Lindt & Sprüngli (OTCPK:LDSVF), and The J. M. Smucker Company (SJM), we see not only the lowest dividend yield but also some of the highest valuation metrics from many points of view. In an environment with issues where virtually all players are hurt and trading at distressed valuations, this company still comes with a very high valuation, and keep in mind that most of these are global players that depend on much more than just the US/North America (vs. ~90% North America for HSY).

Right now, we're paying a premium price for something that's not really worth that much, with a lot of competition that's trading at much better ratios. But sure, a recovery in the industry should be a recovery for HSY as well, although I believe their potential is significantly lower than that of peers such as GIS, both in terms of stock price appreciation and dividends.

Valuation

Based on H1 2025, seasonality in H2, and their CAPEX expectations, I'll estimate that they can make around $850 million in FCF this year. I'll expect some improvements in the environment and the company getting back to $1 billion in 2026 and back to ~$1.25 billion in 2027, followed by a continued solid 7.5% CAGR until 2030 thanks to their expansion potential, rate cuts, and an overall better environment (note: this is below their five-year EPS CAGR of ~15.5%, for example, to take into account the business maturing plus competition adapting to the environment), followed by a 4.5% CAGR for the subsequent five years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate. With $4.73 billion in net debt based on the latest report, 202.7 million shares outstanding, and a WACC of 7% (above my estimated 6.62%, based on a cost of equity of ~7.1% and cost of debt of ~4.7% for a better margin of safety), this returns an enterprise value of $31.12 billion. After subtracting net debt, this gives an equity value of $26.39 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of ~$130.21.

Conclusions

Overall, although I see potential in Hershey to recover together with the broader food industry, I rate them a Hold as a result of the valuation with high expectations and dependency on the North American market and chocolate. These are tied significantly to politics, with both internal issues (Make America Healthy Again policies) and external factors (tariffs) being possible headwinds, although the industry's tailwinds and the company's potential to expand globally remain attractive.
